question: Does the generated text explicitly identify the knot being taught as a 'Bowline' knot?

Does the tutorial text include instructions for all four essential phases of the Bowline: (1) forming a loop, (2) passing the working end up through the loop, (3) going around the standing part, and (4) passing back down through the loop?
Does the text explicitly distinguish between the 'working end' (or rabbit/tag end) and the 'standing part' (or tree) to prevent user confusion?
Are the textual instructions written in clear, imperative language that is free from logical contradictions or ambiguous terminology?
Do the generated images depict a 'thick rope' suitable for camping as requested, rather than thin string, twine, or wire?
Are all generated images 'close-ups' that focus specifically on the rope strands and knot structure?
Are the images free from severe AI artifacts such as the rope 'melting' into itself, impossible clipping (rope passing through itself like a ghost), or distorted hands?
Does the image corresponding to the 'initial loop' step clearly show a single loop formed on the standing part, matching the text description?
Does the image for the 'threading' step accurately show the working end passing UP through the loop (rabbit coming out of the hole) rather than just lying near it?
Does the image for the 'around the standing part' step show the working end wrapping BEHIND the standing part (rabbit going around the tree)?
Does the image for the 'back through the loop' step show the working end passing back DOWN through the original loop (rabbit going back into the hole)?
Do the images clearly distinguish the depth of rope crossings (showing which strand is over and which is under) to provide actual instructional value?
Is the rope consistent in color, thickness, and material texture across all images in the sequence?
Is there at least one illustrative image provided for every distinct step of the knot-tying process described in the text?
Are images placed immediately adjacent to the corresponding text step they illustrate to facilitate easy cross-referencing?
Do the images show a logical chronological progression where the rope configuration in one step naturally leads to the next?
Does the final image depict a structurally correct Bowline knot (identifiable by a fixed loop locked by a collar) rather than a slip knot or random tangle?
Does the tutorial include a final step explaining or showing the tightening/dressing of the knot to ensure it is secure?
